quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "t-distribution"?

options.text: μ = np; the expected number of successes in n trials
A study in which the researcher deliberately imposes one or more treatments on subjects to observe the effect on a response variable
A symmetric, bell-shaped distribution with heavier tails than the normal, parameterized by degrees of freedom (df = n-1 for one sample)
The square root of variance; measures the typical distance of observations from the mean in the original units of measurement

quiz_questions.explanation: "t-distribution" is defined as: A symmetric, bell-shaped distribution with heavier tails than the normal, parameterized by degrees of freedom (df = n-1 for one sample). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Degrees of freedom (df)"?

options.text: The claim we suspect is true and are trying to find evidence for; can be one-sided () or two-sided (≠)
t*·(s/√n); the maximum likely difference between x̄ and μ at the stated confidence level
df = n - 1 for a single sample; controls the shape of the t-distribution—more df means closer to normal
A smooth curve that models the overall shape of a distribution; the total area under the curve equals 1 and represents probability

quiz_questions.explanation: "Degrees of freedom (df)" is defined as: df = n - 1 for a single sample; controls the shape of the t-distribution—more df means closer to normal. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "t-statistic"?

options.text: n = (t*·s/ME)²; the sample size needed to achieve a desired margin of error, often requiring an initial pilot study to estimate s
Tests H₀: μ₁ = μ₂ (or μ₁ - μ₂ = 0) using t = (x̄₁ - x̄₂)/√(s₁²/n₁ + s₂²/n₂)
A variable associated with both the explanatory variable and the response variable that distorts the apparent relationship between them
t = (x̄ - μ₀)/(s/√n); replaces the z-statistic when σ is unknown, using sample standard deviation s in the denominator

quiz_questions.explanation: "t-statistic" is defined as: t = (x̄ - μ₀)/(s/√n); replaces the z-statistic when σ is unknown, using sample standard deviation s in the denominator. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Heavy tails"?

options.text: t-distributions have more probability in the tails than the normal, producing wider confidence intervals and larger critical values for small samples
The probability of correctly rejecting H₀ when Hₐ is true; Power = 1 - β; increased by larger n, larger effect size, or larger α
A variable associated with both the explanatory variable and the response variable that distorts the apparent relationship between them
The distribution of counts for every combination of categories in a two-way table, expressed as proportions of the grand total

quiz_questions.explanation: "Heavy tails" is defined as: t-distributions have more probability in the tails than the normal, producing wider confidence intervals and larger critical values for small samples. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Robustness"?

options.text: The probability of correctly rejecting H₀ when Hₐ is true; Power = 1 - β; increased by larger n, larger effect size, or larger α
t-procedures are robust to moderate non-normality for large samples but can be misleading for small samples from highly skewed distributions
Failing to reject H₀ when Hₐ is actually true; probability denoted β
A study in which the researcher deliberately imposes one or more treatments on subjects to observe the effect on a response variable

quiz_questions.explanation: "Robustness" is defined as: t-procedures are robust to moderate non-normality for large samples but can be misleading for small samples from highly skewed distributions. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A symmetric, bell-shaped distribution with heavier tails than the normal, parameterized by degrees of freedom (df = n-1 for one sample)"?

options.text: Standard error of x̄
Independence requirement
t-test for slope
t-distribution

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "t-distribution".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "df = n - 1 for a single sample; controls the shape of the t-distribution—more df means closer to normal"?

options.text: Interpretation of slope CI
Mutually exclusive events
Degrees of freedom (df)
Standard error of x̄

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Degrees of freedom (df)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "t = (x̄ - μ₀)/(s/√n); replaces the z-statistic when σ is unknown, using sample standard deviation s in the denominator"?

options.text: t-statistic
Standardized residual
Establishing causation
Population regression model

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "t-statistic".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "t-distributions have more probability in the tails than the normal, producing wider confidence intervals and larger critical values for small samples"?

options.text: Standardized conditions
Heavy tails
False positive/negative
Effect size

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Heavy tails".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "t-procedures are robust to moderate non-normality for large samples but can be misleading for small samples from highly skewed distributions"?

options.text: Robustness
Mean difference (d̄)
Independent events
Retrospective vs. prospective study

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Robustness".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about t-Distributions is accurate?

options.text: Central Limit Theorem (CLT) states that regardless of the population's shape, the sampling distribution of x̄ approaches a normal distribution as sample size n increases (generally n ≥ 30 is sufficient).
Confidence Intervals for Slopes estimate the true population slope β₁ using b₁ ± t*·SE(b₁) with df = n-2.
Confidence Intervals for Means estimate an unknown population mean μ using x̄ ± t*(s/√n), where t* comes from the t-distribution with df = n-1.
t-Distributions replace the normal distribution when the population standard deviation σ is unknown and must be estimated by the sample standard deviation s.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes t-Distributions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of t-Distributions?

options.text: Central Limit Theorem (CLT) states that regardless of the population's shape, the sampling distribution of x̄ approaches a normal distribution as sample size n increases (generally n ≥ 30 is sufficient).
The probability distribution table lists all possible values and their probabilities (which must sum to 1), enabling calculation of the mean μ = E(X) = Σ[x·P(x)] and variance σ² = Σ[(x-μ)²·P(x)].
The t-distribution with n-1 degrees of freedom has thicker tails than the standard normal, reflecting the additional uncertainty from estimating σ.
Chi-Square Test for Independence tests whether two categorical variables are associated in a single population.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes t-Distributions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about t-Distributions?

options.text: As degrees of freedom increase, the t-distribution approaches the standard normal, explaining why large-sample procedures using z and small-sample procedures using t give similar results for large n.

The binomial distribution counts the number of successes in a fixed number of independent trials with constant success probability: P(X=k) = C(n,k)p^k(1-p)^(n-k), with mean np and standard deviation √(np(1-p)).
Measures of Variability quantify the spread or dispersion of data, describing how much individual observations deviate from the center.

Data Collection and Types of Variables forms the foundation of statistical analysis by distinguishing between categorical variables (which assign labels or categories) and quantitative variables (which take numerical measurements).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes t-Distributions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of t-Distributions?

options.text: Central Limit Theorem (CLT) states that regardless of the population's shape, the sampling distribution of x̄ approaches a normal distribution as sample size n increases (generally n ≥ 30 is sufficient).
Linear Regression and Inference extends exploratory regression to formal inference about the population regression line β₀ + β₁x.
t-Distributions replace the normal distribution when the population standard deviation σ is unknown and must be estimated by the sample standard deviation s.
Controlling includes keeping conditions identical across groups, standardizing procedures, and using control groups as baselines for comparison.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes t-Distributions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on t-Distributions?

options.text: The t-distribution with n-1 degrees of freedom has thicker tails than the standard normal, reflecting the additional uncertainty from estimating σ.

The four-step process involves stating hypotheses, checking conditions (random sample, 10% condition, np₀ ≥ 10 and n(1-p₀) ≥ 10), calculating the test statistic and p-value, and stating a conclusion in context.
Tree diagrams and two-way tables provide systematic visual methods for computing conditional probabilities in complex scenarios.
The p-value measures the probability of observing data as extreme as or more extreme than the sample result, assuming H₀ is true.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes t-Distributions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of t-Distributions?

options.text: A nutritionist testing whether the mean sodium content of a product exceeds 500 mg using a sample of 15 packages and a one-sample t-test
Public health officials testing whether the proportion of residents with elevated lead levels differs from the national rate to justify environmental remediation
Modeling population growth of bacteria over time using exponential transformation, then predicting colony size at future time points
Environmental monitoring using t-procedures to test whether mean pollutant concentrations exceed safety standards when only sample data (not population σ) are available

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of t-Distributions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of t-Distributions?

options.text: Medical trials comparing average recovery times between a new surgical technique and the standard procedure across two independent patient groups
Hospital administrators constructing a 95% confidence interval for mean patient wait time to evaluate whether current staffing levels meet service standards
Clinical trials with small patient groups using t-distribution critical values for confidence intervals, which are wider than z-intervals to account for imprecise σ estimates
Online survey nonresponse bias where only highly motivated or opinionated individuals choose to respond, skewing results toward extreme views

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of t-Distributions.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is t-Distributions applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Student performance analysis in a classroom of 25 students using t-distribution (df = 24) rather than z-distribution because the true population standard deviation is unknown
Modeling customer arrival times at a store using appropriate probability distributions to optimize staffing schedules
Quality control using CLT to construct control charts for process means even when individual measurements follow a skewed distribution
Public health research testing whether smoking status (smoker/non-smoker) is independent of lung disease status (yes/no) in a random sample of adults

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of t-Distributions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of t-Distributions?

options.text: Environmental monitoring using t-procedures to test whether mean pollutant concentrations exceed safety standards when only sample data (not population σ) are available
Analyzing the power relationship between body mass and metabolic rate in animals using log-log transformation
Comparing median household income (resistant to billionaire outliers) versus mean income when evaluating economic policy and wealth distribution
A/B testing in technology companies randomly assigning users to different website designs to determine which produces higher engagement

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of t-Distributions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows t-Distributions in practice?

options.text: Agricultural experiments blocking by field section (soil quality, sunlight exposure) before randomly assigning crop varieties within each block
Identifying confounding variables in public health studies, such as separating the effect of diet from exercise on heart disease rates
News organizations reporting poll results with margin of error ('52% favor the measure ± 3 percentage points') derived from the confidence interval formula
Clinical trials with small patient groups using t-distribution critical values for confidence intervals, which are wider than z-intervals to account for imprecise σ estimates

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of t-Distributions.
